coredata.dc:title: Gasoline residue-prepared carbon dots embedded metal–organic frameworks for selective and sensitive detection of sulfide ions
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yi
Hu, Kelin
Yuan, Bo
Zhu, Xinghua
Chen, Xi
Huang, Ke
coredata.dc:description: Sulfide ions (S2-) are well-known and hazardous pollutants that are commonly found in natural water and wastewater. Their excessive presence can lead to environmental problems and pose a risk to living systems and human health. Therefore, the development of affordable and accurate methods for detecting trace amounts of S2- has become a topic of great interest. This study presents a novel approach to constructing highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using gasoline residue from combustion. The CDs were then embedded into a zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) crystal at room temperature, resulting in the creation of a CDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe for S2- detection. The sensing mechanism was based on the fact that the encapsulation of CDs into ZIF-8 can restrain its fluorescent emission, while fluorescence enhancement could be observed by adding S2- due to the S2- can react with the metal node (Zn2+) of ZIF-8 to release the CDs. The sensing platform achieved a low limit of detection (LOD) of 330 nM and a relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 7) of 0.7% under optimized conditions. The platform was successfully applied to quantify S2- in water and gasoline samples, indicating its potential for practical use. The method proposed in this study is rapid, direct, and sensitive for S2- assay with favorable experimental conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S2-
CDs@ZIF-8
Fluorescence
Ignitable liquid
Gasoline residue

We would like to thank Home for Researchers (www.home-for-researchers.com).

We would like to thank Home for Researchers (www.home-for-researchers.com).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD pattern (A) and TEM image (B) of the CDs prepared from gasoline.
(A) XRD patterns, (B) elemental surveys from XPS of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8. SEM images of (C) ZIF-8, and (D) CDs@ZIF-8.
(A) Fluorescence peaks of CDs@ZIF-8 ZIF-8, and CDs. (B) F/F0 of CDs@ZIF-8 ZIF-8, and CDs mixed with 100 μM and 1000 μM S2-. Error bars were estimated from three replicate measurements.

(A) The range of F/F0 signal for various concentrations of S2- from 1 to 400 μM). (B) The specificity of the S2- sensing platform with a concentration of 20 μM of S2- and 200 μM of interferences. Error bars were estimated from three replicate measurements.
The schematic diagram of the CDs@ZIF-8 for the detection of S2-.
Comparison of analytical performance with other methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: MOF: metal–organic frameworks;b CDs/PEI/NB: carbon dots conjugated with polyethyleneimine and Nile Blue; c GSH: glutathione; d AE: active ester; e CNT: carbon nanotubes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results of S2- in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean ± SD (n = 3); b: ND: Not detected; c Recovery = (CDetected /CAdded) × 100%.

head.ce:title: Gasoline residue-prepared carbon dots embedded metal–organic frameworks for selective and sensitive detection of sulfide ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sichuan Fire Research Institute of Ministry of Emergency Management, Chengdu, Sichuan 610036, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sichuan Fire Research Institute of Ministry of Emergency Management

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sichuan Fire Research Institute of Ministry of Emergency Management, Chengdu, Sichuan 610036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610068, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610068, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Principle of the encased CDs in ZIF-8 for S2- assay.

Sulfide ions (S2-) are well-known and hazardous pollutants that are commonly found in natural water and wastewater. Their excessive presence can lead to environmental problems and pose a risk to living systems and human health. Therefore, the development of affordable and accurate methods for detecting trace amounts of S2- has become a topic of great interest. This study presents a novel approach to constructing highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using gasoline residue from combustion. The CDs were then embedded into a zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) crystal at room temperature, resulting in the creation of a CDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe for S2- detection. The sensing mechanism was based on the fact that the encapsulation of CDs into ZIF-8 can restrain its fluorescent emission, while fluorescence enhancement could be observed by adding S2- due to the S2- can react with the metal node (Zn2+) of ZIF-8 to release the CDs. The sensing platform achieved a low limit of detection (LOD) of 330 nM and a relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 7) of 0.7% under optimized conditions. The platform was successfully applied to quantify S2- in water and gasoline samples, indicating its potential for practical use. The method proposed in this study is rapid, direct, and sensitive for S2- assay with favorable experimental conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfide ions (S2-) are essential in biological, industrial, and environmental processes and are commonly found in natural water and wastewater [1,2]. However, excessive amounts of S2- can harm the ecological environment and pose a risk to human health [3,4]. Additionally, S2- can protonate to form more toxic HS- and H2S, which can cause discomfort in mucous membranes, loss of consciousness, or even permanent brain damage [5]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a selective and sensitive system for monitoring S2- levels.

In recent times, various methods such as titration [6], electrochemical methods [7,8], chromatography [9,10], and spectroscopic methods (including chemiluminescence [11,12], fluorescence [13–16], UV–vis spectrophotometric and colorimetric methods [17,18]) have been successfully utilized for S2- detection. The fluorescent assay is a popular method for the highly sensitive detection of target analytes in quantitative analysis [19]. Various luminescent substances such as quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs), upconversion nanoparticles, and organic dyes, have been utilized as signal molecules in fluorescent methods [20–22]. Among them, CDs have been widely used as an ideal nanoprobe in fluorescent sensing because of their advantageous properties such as low toxicity, easy preparation, good water solubility, good chemical inertia, and good biocompatibility [23,24].

Metal-organic framework (MOF) is a novel type of porous material composed of organic groups and metal ions [25–27]. Due to their diverse structures, high surface area, and various functionalities, MOFs have found widespread use in biosensing applications [28,29]. Recently, MOF can be used as a luminous guest host to fabricate nanoprobes [30,31]. For example, A fluorescence recovery method was effectively utilized for detecting ATP by releasing fluorescent RhB from RhB/ZIF-90 [32]. Chen et al. prepared luminescent composites of CDs@ZIF-8 and used them to detect dopamine (DA) with high sensitivity [33]. In our previous study, we successfully developed a method for directly measuring butyrylcholinesterase using CDs@ZIF-8 [34]. The reaction between targets and MOFs results in the release of luminescent substances, leading to significant fluorescence. Therefore, it has great significance to combine MOFs and CDs for S2- detection due to their photochemical stability and tunable emission lights.

In this study, we have prepared fluorescent CDs using gasoline residue after combustion and embedded them into ZIF-8 crystal at room temperature to create a CDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe for detecting S2-. The encapsulation of CDs@ZIF-8 restrained its fluorescent emission, but when S2- reacted with the metal node (Zn2+) of ZIF-8, it could destroy ZIF-8 and release the CDs, resulting in fluorescence enhancement (as shown in Scheme 1 ). The mechanisms for S2- sensing were explored through a series of characterizations and experiments. The fluorescent nanoprobe was found to provide accurate results when used to quantitatively detect S2- in real gasoline and water samples, with the advantages of being low-cost, simple, and sensitive.
The details of the apparatus and reagents utilized can be found in the Supporting Information (SI).

In accordance with previous research, CDs were prepared using an innovative method [35]. A crucible was used to completely combust 25 mL of processed gasoline. After the blank granule was cooled to room temperature, it was washed down from the crucible wall and the volume was maintained at 20 mL with anhydrous ethanol. The resulting suspension was heated at 150 °C for 6 h in a 50 mL autoclave. To remove large particles, the tawny CDs solutions were filtrated using 0.22 μm membranes. Subsequently, 12 mL of the filtered solution was mixed with 3 mL of 0.6 mg mL−1 NH2-PEG-NH2 / anhydrous ethanol solution and kept in the dark for 48 h. Following the mix with 12 mL 0.1 M Na2CO3 solution, the mixture was left in the dark for 24 h. The resulting product was filtrated by a 0.22 μm membrane and reposited at 4 °C before use.

The synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8 was carried out according to our previous work [34]. Specifically, 0.5 mL of CDs solution was added to a 2 mL solution of 0.4 M N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and 2-Methylimidazole (2-MIm). This was then mixed with a 2 mL solution of 0.2 M Zn(CH3COOH)2·2H2O in DMF, which was added quickly to the previous mixture. The prepared material obtained through centrifugation separation underwent washing with DMF and ethanol before 60 °C drying. Pure ZIF-8 was successfully obtained without the need for additional CDs.

Emulsion breaking extraction was utilized to isolate S2- from gasoline acquired from the Sichuan Fire Research Institute under the Ministry of Emergency Management [36]. To begin, an emulsion was created by combining 4800 μL of gasoline with 600 μL extractant solution containing 4% (m/v) Triton X-100 and 15% HNO3. The mixtures were sonicated for 15 min to ensure a complete reaction between the extractant and gasoline and then separated by 5 min 10000 rpm centrifugation [37,38] Following centrifugation, the upper aqueous phase, which contained the extracted S2- from the gasoline along with a small quantity of emulsifier, was aspirated with care and then diluted with ultrapure water for further analysis. The water sample was collected on the campus and filtrated before analysis.

0.1 mg mL−1 of the nanocomposites were prepared before usage. To initiate the reaction, 0.5 mL of CDs@ZIF-8 solution was mixed with 0.5 mL of various content of S2-. The obtained solutions were incubated at 37 °C for 15 min, and fluorescence was measured after excitation at 370 nm.

The CDs was prepared using the gasoline residue after combustion. Firstly, fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis were performed, with a fluorescence emission of 446 nm observed at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. UV–vis absorption spectrum peak at 370 nm was measured and corresponded with the n-π* switching of C = O and C = N (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 1 A, the XRD pattern exhibited a broad and obtuse peak from 20° to 30° for CDs, indicating the irregular arrangement of C atoms [39]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1B) demonstrated the united size (around 6.5 nm) of CDs and the proposed CDs were well monodisperse.

To validate the successful integration of ZIF-8 and CDs and ensure the integrity of the ZIF-8 structure, XRD, XPS, and SEM techniques were employed. Fig. 2 A demonstrates that XRD images of both CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8 were consistent with the simulated pattern (CCDC 602542). Diffraction peaks of thes nanocomposites were highly similar with those of ZIF-8, revealing that the crystal structure remained intact after the introduction of CDs. The XPS survey scans in Fig. 2B showed the presence of N, O, C, and Zn of CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. S2A) revealed the binding energies of C-N and C-C bonds at 285.7 eV and 284.6 eV, respectively [40,41]. The position of C-N bonds in 2-MIm was identified at 398.6 eV in the N 1 s region (Fig. S2B) [42]. The O1s (Fig. S2C) region displayed a prominent peak at 513.2 eV, indicating the presence of O-H bonds [43]. Fig. S2D displays the XPS spectra of Zn 2p with the two primary peaks at 1044.8 eV and 1021.8 eV corresponding to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, respectively. Additionally, the specific spin-energy separation (ΔE = 23.0 eV) is consistent with Zn2+ [44].

The introduction of CDs into ZIF-8 was confirmed through SEM images. ZIF-8 exhibited a regular dodecahedral shape as shown in Fig. 2C. On the other hand, Fig. 2D displayed a fuller crystal structure with sharper angles of CDs@ZIF-8 due to the presence of CDs wrapped in ZIF-8. The research findings indicate that CDs can be successfully loaded into ZIF-8 without affecting its crystal structure or polyhedral morphology.

As demonstrated in Fig. 3 A, the CDs revealed a strong fluorescence intensity (FI), whereas the ZIF-8 had a bare FI of around 446 nm. Integration of CDs and ZIF-8 can significantly decrease the FI. In addition, Fig. 3B shows that there was no noticeable change in F/F0 (where F0 and F represented the FI in the absence and presence of S2-, respectively) upon adding S2- (100 μM) to ZIF-8 or CDs. However, F/F0 continuously increased when CDs@ZIF-8 was mixed with increasing concentrations of S2- (100 μM to 1000 μM), suggesting the possibility of using this sensing strategy for the determination of S2- via ZIF-encapsulated CDs.

To achieve optimal S2- sensing performance, the experimental conditions were carefully optimized. The volume of CDs used for synthesizing CDs@ZIF-8 and the reaction time for ZIF synthesis were found to have a significant impact on S2- sensing. The results indicate that the best signals (F/F0) were obtained with 500 µL CDs volume (see Fig. S3A). Furthermore, Fig. S3B shows that 10 min reaction time was sufficient for ZIF-8 preparation. Finally, the investigation of CDs@ZIF-8 concentration revealed that the maximum F/F0 was observed at 0.1 mg mL−1 (see Fig. S3C). The study also examined the reaction time of S2- and CDs@ZIF-8. According to Fig. S3D, the F/F0 increased before 15 min and then decreased, possibly due to the aggregation of generated ZnS. Ultimately, 0.1 mg mL−1 of CDs@ZIF-8 containing 500 µL CDs solution was utilized to react with S2- for 15 min in next experiments.

To assess the proposed method's anti-interference ability, multiple cations (Na+, K+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Al3+, and Ca2+) and anions (Cl-, NO3 – H2PO4 -) were examined. As shown in Fig. 4B, even when their concentration was increased by 10-fold to S2-, no significant signal change was observed for these cations and anions. This indicates that the proposed platform has a high selectivity for the determination of S2-.

To reveal the practicability of the developed sensing platform, three kinds of real samples including gasoline, tap water, and pond water were measured. However, S2- was not detected in any of the samples. As shown in Table 2 , the standard addition method was utilized and good recoveries (from 96 to 105%) were obtained, validating the feasibility of the proposed method for real analysis.

In general, a simple and rapid off–on fluorescent method was developed for the detection of S2-. The analytical platform was prepared rapidly at room temperature and exhibited a linear response from 1 to 40 μM with a LOD. The analytical performance for S2- assay was comparable to other recorded FL methods, and it did not require time-consuming or complicated modification, and it has made good use of waste materials for CDs preparation. This study presents a promising alternative approach to S2- sensing and has the potential to advance the methodology of S2- quantification in the future. Furthermore, this strategy provides a novel approach for the analysis of other analytes in Gasoline samples with the guest host strategy.

Yi Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Kelin Hu: Investigation. Bo Yuan: Investigation. Xinghua Zhu: Investigation. Xi Chen: Validation. Ke Huang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2023NSFSC1090), the Basic Research Funds Team Program of Sichuan Fire Research Institute of Ministry of Emergency Management (T20238801), and the Foundation of Sichuan Normal University (No. SYJS2022011 and ZZYQ2022002). We would like to thank Home for Researchers (www.home-for-researchers.com).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109035.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Use of fluorescent DNA-templated gold/silver nanoclusters for the detection of sulfide ions
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.-Y. Chen, G.-Y. Lan, H.-T. Chang, Use of Fluorescent DNA-Templated Gold/Silver Nanoclusters for the Detection of Sulfide Ions, Anal. Chem., 83 (2011) 9450-9455.

Water Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Miloshova, D. Baltes, E. Bychkov, New chalcogenide glass chemical sensors for S2- and dissolved H2S monitoring, Water Sci. Technol., 47 (2003) 135-140.

Analyst

J. Clin. Periodontol.

Appl. Microbiol. Biotechnol.

Microchim. Acta

Luminescence

Actuat, B-Chem

Microchem. J.
